description: A comprehensive training program for building autonomous drone systems that capture cinematic aerial footage for movies, commercials, live events, and virtual production. The program covers all four engineering tracks—AI, MLOps, Data Engineering, and Edge AI—applied specifically to creative cinematography, enabling autonomous camera movement, subject tracking, complex shot programming, and real‑time visual effects integration.
learning_objectives: Understand cinematic techniques: composition, camera movement, lighting, and storytelling through aerial perspectives
Build and train AI models for subject tracking (actors, vehicles), scene understanding, and aesthetic shot evaluation
Create data pipelines for shot logging, metadata management, and integration with post‑production workflows (VFX, color grading)
Deploy optimized models on edge hardware for real‑time tracking, smooth camera gimbal control, and collision avoidance during complex moves
Implement autonomous shot execution: pre‑programmed flight paths, dynamic subject following, multi‑drone coordination
Design safe flight operations on sets with actors, crew, and equipment, including emergency procedures and redundancy
Comply with film industry safety standards (e.g., Section 107, motion picture & television industry guidelines)

lessons.title: Cinematic Language and Drone Aesthetics
lessons.content: Study core cinematography principles: framing (rule of thirds, leading lines), camera movement (dolly, crane, tracking, whip pan), focal length and depth of field, lighting (golden hour, dramatic shadows), and pacing. Understand how drones enable unique shots: overhead establishing shots, low‑altitude tracking, reveal shots (ascending from behind obstacles), orbit shots, and hyperlapses. Analyze iconic drone shots from films and commercials. Learn about storytelling through aerial perspective: establishing scale, revealing geography, creating emotional impact.

lessons.title: Operational Scenarios and Requirements
lessons.content: Analyze scenarios: feature film sequences (chase scenes, sweeping landscapes), television commercials (product reveals), live sports (tracking athletes), music videos (dynamic choreography), virtual production (LED wall integration), and real‑time broadcast (concerts, award shows). Define requirements: high‑end cameras (RED Komodo, Sony Venice, or DJI Inspire with Zenmuse X9), high‑frame‑rate capture, low‑latency video transmission (for director’s monitor), precise flight control (RTK GPS, vision positioning), quiet operation for on‑set sound recording, dual‑operator setup (pilot + camera operator), and redundancy for reliability.

modules.title: Module 2: AI Engineer – Cinematic Vision and Tracking

lessons.title: Subject Detection and Tracking
lessons.content: Train an object detection and tracking model (e.g., YOLO + DeepSORT) to follow actors, vehicles, or objects in real time. Use datasets of people in motion, vehicles, and sports scenarios. Implement visual servoing: the drone maintains a specified relative position (e.g., 10 m behind, 5 m above) while tracking the subject. Output is a target position for the flight controller. For multiple subjects, allow operator selection.
lessons.code_example:
from ultralytics import YOLO
from deep_sort_realtime.deepsort_tracker import DeepSort
yolo = YOLO('yolov8n.pt')
tracker = DeepSort()
# per frame: detect, track, compute relative drone target

lessons.title: Scene Understanding for Cinematic Framing
lessons.content: Train a model to analyze the scene and suggest optimal framing: detect key elements (subject, horizon, leading lines), evaluate current composition against aesthetic rules (e.g., rule of thirds, headroom), and output adjustments to camera gimbal and drone position. This enables semi‑autonomous “cinematic assistant” features. Use a dataset of professionally framed shots labeled with composition scores.

lessons.title: Obstacle Avoidance for Dynamic Sets
lessons.content: Train a model to detect and avoid obstacles (cranes, lighting rigs, other drones, crew) while maintaining smooth cinematic motion. Use 360° cameras or LiDAR. Implement predictive avoidance: anticipate the subject’s path and pre‑plan clear trajectories. Ensure avoidance does not result in jerky movements—smooth re‑planning is essential.
lessons.code_example:
import numpy as np
# Use potential fields or model predictive control for smooth avoidance

lessons.title: Visual Effects (VFX) Metadata Generation
lessons.content: Generate metadata for post‑production: camera position, orientation, lens parameters, lighting conditions, and tracked markers for CGI integration. Train models to detect and log tracking markers (fiducials) placed on set. Output a sidecar file (e.g., Alembic, FBX) that can be imported into VFX software (Unreal Engine, Maya).
lessons.code_example:
import json
metadata = {
    'frame': frame_num,
    'camera_position': [x, y, z],
    'camera_rotation': [roll, pitch, yaw],
    'focal_length': 24,
    'tracking_markers': marker_positions
}

modules.title: Module 3: Data Engineer – Shot Management and Post‑Production Pipeline

lessons.title: Shot Logging with PostGIS
lessons.content: Store metadata for each shot: take number, location (geometry), start/end time, camera settings, subject, director’s notes, and VFX data. Use spatial queries to retrieve shots by scene, location, or subject. Integrate with production scheduling software (e.g., Movie Magic).

lessons.title: STAC for Cinematic Footage Archives
lessons.content: Catalog all drone footage using STAC. Each item includes bounding box, datetime, camera model, frame rate, resolution, and creative tags (e.g., “sunset”, “tracking shot”). Enable fast retrieval for editors and VFX teams.

lessons.title: Integration with Editorial and VFX Workflows
lessons.content: Build APIs to push shot metadata and camera tracking data to post‑production tools (Avid, DaVinci Resolve, Unreal Engine). Automate creation of edit lists, camera solves, and lighting references. Use industry‑standard formats (ALE, CDL, FBX).
lessons.code_example:
import unreal
# Use Unreal Engine Python API to import camera track

modules.title: Module 4: Edge AI Engineer – Autonomous Cinematography Drone

lessons.title: Hardware Configuration for Cinematic Drones
lessons.content: Select sensors: professional cinema camera (gimbal‑stabilized, interchangeable lenses), high‑resolution video transmitter (e.g., DJI Transmission), dual‑operator setup (pilot and camera operator), RTK GPS for precise positioning, obstacle sensors (stereo cameras, time‑of‑flight). Use a drone with low noise (quiet propellers, aerodynamic design) to avoid interfering with audio capture. Connect to Jetson Orin Nano for AI‑assisted tracking and composition.

lessons.title: DeepStream Pipeline for Real‑Time Tracking Overlay
lessons.content: Build a DeepStream pipeline that runs subject detection and tracking on the camera feed, overlaying a tracking box and predicted trajectory on the operator’s monitor. Also display composition guides (e.g., rule‑of‑thirds grid, horizon level). Use hardware acceleration for low latency (< 50 ms).

lessons.title: ROS 2 Integration for Autonomous Camera Moves
lessons.content: Create a ROS 2 node that executes pre‑programmed shot sequences (e.g., “dolly in from 50 m to 10 m while orbiting 45°”). Use MAVROS to command position and velocity. For subject tracking, compute relative target pose and publish velocity commands to both drone and gimbal. Implement smooth motion profiles (acceleration‑limited) to avoid jerky shots.

modules.title: Module 5: ML Platform Engineer – Continuous Improvement for Cinematic AI

lessons.title: Active Learning for Uncommon Subjects
lessons.content: Collect tracking data where the model struggled (e.g., fast‑moving vehicles, partially occluded actors). Send clips to editors for annotation (bounding boxes). Retrain tracking models to improve performance on the specific subjects and environments used on set.
lessons.code_example:
uncertain = [frame for frame in shot if track_confidence < 0.6]
cvat.create_task('cinematography_active_learning', uncertain)

lessons.title: Kubeflow Pipelines for Model Retraining
lessons.content: Set up a Kubeflow pipeline that retrains tracking and composition models after each production, incorporating new footage and annotations. Deploy improved models to drones before the next shoot. Version models by lens, environment, or subject type.
lessons.code_example:
from kfp import dsl
@dsl.pipeline
def cinema_model_retraining():
    data_prep = dsl.ContainerOp(...)
    train = dsl.ContainerOp(...).after(data_prep)
    evaluate = dsl.ContainerOp(...).after(train)
    deploy = dsl.ContainerOp(...).after(evaluate)

lessons.title: Monitoring Model Drift with Lighting and Set Changes
lessons.content: Use Evidently AI to compare tracking confidence and composition scores across different lighting conditions (day exterior, night, interior) and set designs. Drift may trigger retraining with representative footage.

lessons.title: Hardware‑in‑the‑Loop (HIL) with Virtual Production
lessons.content: Use NVIDIA Isaac Sim or Unreal Engine with high‑fidelity environments, animated characters, and moving vehicles. Simulate complex shots (e.g., car chase through city). Connect real Pixhawk and Jetson. Test tracking accuracy, smoothness of motion, and obstacle avoidance. Evaluate the final rendered footage for cinematic quality.
lessons.code_example: Isaac Sim with ROS2 bridge; drone flies pre‑programmed path; Jetson runs tracking; virtual camera records output for review.

lessons.title: Latency Profiling for Real‑Time Monitoring
lessons.content: Profile end‑to‑end latency from camera capture to monitor display using Nsight Systems. For live director’s monitor, latency should be < 100 ms. Optimize by using hardware‑accelerated encoding and low‑latency transmission protocols.

modules.title: Module 7: Safety, Regulation, and Industry Standards

lessons.title: On‑Set Safety Protocols
lessons.content: Follow motion picture industry safety guidelines: maintain safe distances from actors and crew, use propeller guards, conduct pre‑flight briefings, designate safety observers. Implement emergency stop mechanisms. Coordinate with stunt coordinators for action scenes. Ensure drones are insured and pilots certified (FAA Part 107).

lessons.title: Regulatory Considerations
lessons.content: Operate under Part 107 (US) or equivalent. For flights over people (common on sets), obtain waivers or use drones with low‑risk classification. Follow local noise ordinances and filming permits. Use geofencing to avoid restricted airspace near airports or stadiums.

lessons.title: Data Security and IP Protection
lessons.content: Protect unreleased footage and metadata. Use encrypted storage, access controls, and secure transfer protocols. Implement non‑disclosure agreements with all crew. Ensure that AI models do not leak proprietary creative data.

capstone_project.name: Autonomous Cinematography Drone for a Chase Scene
capstone_project.description: Build and test a drone system (in HIL or a controlled outdoor area) that autonomously executes a pre‑planned cinematic shot: tracking a moving vehicle or actor along a 200 m path while maintaining a specific framing (e.g., 3/4 rear view, constant distance). The system must demonstrate smooth motion, subject tracking with < 5 m deviation, and obstacle avoidance (simulated obstacles). Output a video clip with metadata suitable for VFX integration. Achieve tracking success > 90% and motion smoothness (jerk) below a threshold.
deliverables: ROS 2 nodes for tracking, path planning, and smooth control
HIL simulation report with tracking accuracy and motion quality metrics
Safety and regulatory compliance plan for on‑set operations

resources.name: Unreal Engine Virtual Production
resources.description: Integration of drone footage with VFX
